plainMeaning: Like Mount Meru, the holy community has lovely caves of compassion where lion-like great sages dwell, with a hundred mineral-streams flowing and caverns aglow with jewels and shining herbs.
recitation: jīvadayā sundara kandaru, dariya muṇivara maïnda iṇaṇassa | heu saya dhāu pagalanta, rayaṇa dittosahi guhassa ||

wordByWord.etymology: jīva (living being) + dayā (mercy, compassion); the foundational Jain ethic of ahiṁsā expressed as active kindness
wordByWord.notes: Here the 'material' out of which the mountain's beautiful caves are imagined — compassion is what makes the Saṅgha-Meru's recesses lovely.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. sundara, 'fair, handsome'; qualifies the caves (kandara)
Skt./Pkt. kandara, a hollow or grotto in a hill; final -u is the Apabhraṁśa nominative
wordByWord.notes: Pairs with guhā in the second line; the cave is the verse's governing image.

wordByWord.etymology: Pkt. dariya < Skt. darpita, 'made proud, exultant'; a stock epithet of lions
wordByWord.notes: Modifies maïnda; the 'proud lions' are simultaneously the fearless muṇivaras.

wordByWord.etymology: muṇi (Skt. muni, silent ascetic) + vara (best, excellent)
wordByWord.notes: The simile's pivot: the great munis ARE the lions roaming the Saṅgha-Meru's caves.

wordByWord.etymology: Pkt. maïnda < Skt. mṛgendra, 'lord of beasts' (mṛga + indra)
genitive sg. in -ssa; most plausibly a Pkt. form of *(maïnda-)ayaṇa < Skt. āyatana, 'abode, dwelling-place,' i.e. 'of the lions' den'
wordByWord.notes: Reading uncertain; the book's spelling is preserved verbatim. It functions as one of the two parallel genitives (with guhassa) describing the Meru-mountain. See notes.

wordByWord.etymology: Pkt. heu < Skt. hetu, 'cause, reason, source'; here the spring/source from which the mineral-streams issue
wordByWord.notes: A quiet double meaning: the mountain's mineral 'sources' also evoke the Saṅgha as the 'cause' (hetu) of merit.

wordByWord.etymology: Pkt. saya < Skt. śata, 'hundred'; here hyperbolic for 'manifold'
Pkt. dhāu < Skt. dhātu, the reddish/ochre mineral veins streaking a mountain's flanks
wordByWord.notes: A standard ornament of Meru-description: jewel-bright mineral pigments.

wordByWord.etymology: present participle, Pkt. pagalanta < Skt. pragalat (root gal, 'to drip, flow'); cf. pragalita
Pkt. rayaṇa < Skt. ratna; in compound with the following word
ditta (Skt. dīpta, 'blazing, radiant') + osahi (Skt. oṣadhi, 'herb, healing plant'); the night-glowing herbs of legend that light a cave — taken with rayaṇa, 'jewels and luminous herbs'
wordByWord.notes: Classic Meru trope: caverns illumined without sunlight by gem-light and phosphorescent herbs.

wordByWord.etymology: genitive sg. of Pkt. guhā < Skt. guhā, 'cave, cavern'; parallels iṇaṇassa and the genitives that close every Meru-verse

literalTranslation: [The Saṅgha-Meru,] of the lions'-den with lovely caves of compassion where proud lion-like great sages dwell; of the cave from which a hundred mineral-streams trickle, [a cave] radiant with jewels and glowing herbs.
idiomaticTranslation: Behold the Saṅgha as the great mountain: its caverns lovely with compassion are the den of proud, lion-hearted sages; down its flanks a hundred mineral-springs come streaming, and its grottoes blaze, lit from within by jewels and shining herbs.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This gāthā lingers in the inner recesses of the Saṅgha-mountain. Where v.13's slopes were public — golden cliffs, the Nandana grove — v.14 takes us into the caves (kandara, guhā), and every cave-feature is decoded into a virtue. The 'lovely caves of compassion' (jīvadayā sundara kandaru) make ahiṁsā itself the sheltering hollow of the mountain: the Saṅgha's deepest interior is mercy. Dwelling there are 'proud lions' (dariya maïnda) who are, by apposition, the 'great sages' (muṇivara) — the verse's finest stroke, for the muni's fearless, solitary austerity is exactly the lion's: unhunted, unafraid, sovereign in his cave. From the mountain a hundredfold mineral-streams trickle (heu saya dhāu pagalanta); on the literal Meru these are the ochre dhātu-veins, but heu ('source, cause') lets the line breathe a second sense — the Saṅgha is the wellspring from which countless causes of merit flow down to the world. The closing genitive (rayaṇa dittosahi guhassa) seals the image with light: the cave glows not from the sun but from jewels and luminous night-herbs (dīpta-oṣadhi) set in its own walls — self-illumined, like a community whose darkness is dispelled from within by the three jewels and the inner radiance of knowledge. Note how the verse advances the arc: it prepares the next gāthā's waterfalls of saṁvara that 'trickle' (pagaliya) just as these minerals do — the poet hands the verb forward.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: Two doctrinal points are verse-specific. First, jīvadayā as the very substance of the caves grounds the whole Meru-rūpaka in ahiṁsā: the community's strength is not mere endurance but active compassion. Second, the muni-as-lion figure encodes the ideal of the nirbhaya, self-reliant ascetic — vīrya (spiritual energy) and abhaya (fearlessness) — recalling Mahāvīra's own epithet 'great hero.' The self-illumined cave (jewels and glowing herbs, not borrowed sunlight) is a precise emblem of samyag-jñāna: right knowledge is light arising from within, not received from without — the Saṅgha needs no external lamp.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: For the reciter, this verse turns inward devotion into self-examination: Is the cave of my heart 'lovely with compassion'? Am I a 'lion' — steady and unafraid in restraint — or merely hiding? And do I wait for outer validation, or let the inner jewel of right knowledge give its own light? Chanting it during Pakkhī pratikramaṇa, one resolves that ahiṁsā should line one's innermost recesses.

crossReferences.text: Daśavaikālika 1.1 — 'dhammo maṅgalam ukkiṭṭhaṁ, ahiṁsā saṁjamo tavo' (compassion/ahiṁsā as the supreme dharma)
Ācārāṅga 1.4 — the fearless, lion-like solitary muni
Tattvārtha-sūtra 1.1 — samyag-darśana-jñāna-cāritra as the three jewels (the cave's self-light)

sources.title: Kandara — Prakrit/Sanskrit definitions
sources.consultedFor: Sense of kandara/kandaru as 'mountain cave, grotto' in Prakrit and Sanskrit

sources.title: Mrigendra (mṛga+indra) — definitions
sources.consultedFor: mṛgendra 'lord of beasts/lion' as the source of Pkt. maïnda

sources.title: Dhatu — Sanskrit/Prakrit definitions
sources.consultedFor: dhātu as 'mineral ore, colored earth-element' streaking a mountain

sources.title: Oshadhi — definitions
sources.consultedFor: oṣadhi 'herb/healing plant'; basis of the dīpta-oṣadhi 'glowing herb' trope

sources.title: Nandī-sūtra (Śvetāmbara Āgama)
sources.consultedFor: Identity of the Nandī-sūtra and its opening Saṅgha-Meru praise

notes: The Meru/Mandara simile (preface vv.12–18) is assumed, not restated. Tokenization is secure except ઇણણસ્સ (iṇaṇassa): the book's spelling is preserved verbatim and read here as a genitive parallel to guhassa, most plausibly *(maïnda-)ayaṇassa < āyatana 'abode/den', though it may be a copyist's variant or carry a different base; flagged as uncertain. દરિય is taken as darpita 'proud' (epithet of lions) rather than 'river' (Hindi dariyā), which fits the mountain imagery. હેઉ is read as hetu 'source/cause', yielding the deliberate pun between the mountain's mineral-springs and the Saṅgha as a 'source' of merit; an alternative bare-particle reading is possible. દિત્તોસહિ resolves to dīpta-oṣadhi 'blazing luminous herbs'. Mātrās, anusvāras and conjuncts follow the Anchalgachchha/Vidhipaksha book without normalization; precise wording should be confirmed against a critical edition of the Nandī-sūtra opening, whose Saṅgha-simile sequence varies slightly across recensions.
